title: The Nicene Creed, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 1 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: October Reformation celebration: considering the Nicene Creed
The Creed was written before the Reformation but recovered by it
Historical context: the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) and Arianism
The opening: ‘I believe in one God, the Father Almighty’
The Creed’s role in defining orthodox Trinitarian theology
summary: Opening lesson of an October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, part of the annual Reformation celebration. The Nicene Creed predates the Reformation but was recovered and honored by the Reformers. This first lesson introduces the Creed’s historical context (the Arian controversy and Council of Nicaea, 325 AD) and begins exploring its foundational affirmation of one God and the full deity of Jesus Christ.
transcript:
## Celebrating the Nicene Creed: 1700 Years

This month we are celebrating the 1700th anniversary of the Council of Nicaea and considering the Nicene Creed, which we recite on the first Sunday of each month. Without exaggeration it can be said that every church and every Christian ought to know the Nicene Creed — because the theology it contains is the basic theology of Christianity. If you leave what the creed teaches, you have left Christianity.

The creed is recognized by the Roman Catholic Church, the Eastern Orthodox Church, and every branch of the Protestant church. It is one piece of doctrine that everyone who names himself or herself a Christian embraces.

## A Note on the Text

What we call the Nicene Creed is actually not exactly what the Council of Nicaea produced in 325 AD. What we have today came out of the Council of Constantinople in 381. Technically, it should be called the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed — but that is hard to say, so "Nicene Creed" it is.

The Council of Constantinople in 381 expanded the original 325 creed by including a much longer statement on the Holy Spirit, as well as new wording about the Virgin Mary, the crucifixion, baptism, the church, the resurrection, and the life everlasting.

In 589, at the Council of Toledo, the Western church added the phrase "and the Son" (*filioque*) to the clause about the Holy Spirit proceeding — saying the Spirit proceeds "from the Father *and the Son*." This addition eventually became one of the causes of the Great Schism in 1054 between the Eastern and Western churches. The translation we use includes this addition.

## Why Was the Creed Needed?

By the beginning of the fourth century, the church was still working to articulate the doctrine of the Trinity and define the person of Christ with precision. The church knew that the Father, Son, and Spirit were profoundly the same — and yet in some important way distinct. Christians understood the Son was to be worshiped, as Scripture taught. At the same time, Jesus had been born, lived, and died as a real Jewish man. The church needed time and careful biblical reflection to speak the truth clearly and safeguard it from error.

The Spirit typically leads the church to formulate doctrine through four means: Scripture, time, people using their minds, and — this is significant — **controversy**. Virtually every core doctrine of Christianity was clarified in response to false teaching. Galatians was written because someone said salvation is not by faith alone. 1 Corinthians was written because Christians were living contrary to Scripture. 1 John was written because someone denied the deity of Christ. The New Testament itself is largely a response to controversy.

## Arius and the Crisis

Around 318 AD, a presbyter from Alexandria named **Arius** began teaching his theological views publicly. He was educated, impressive, devout, and sincere. He genuinely wanted everyone to live lives pleasing to the Lord. But Arius did not believe that Jesus was truly God — at least not God in the same way the Father is.

His driving concern was to protect two doctrines: **monotheism** (there is only one God) and the **unity of God** (that one God is undivided). He reasoned that if Jesus was God in the same way the Father is God, and Jesus suffered and died and had limitations, then God himself was weak and changeable. He could not reconcile full divinity with the genuine humanity of Jesus.

Arius was willing to say Jesus was *divine*, but not that he was fully God as the Father is. He thought Jesus must have been created — before time, to be sure, but still created. His famous slogan was: **"There was when he was not"** — meaning there was a moment before the Son existed.

His syllogism ran like this: (1) The Son was begotten of the Father; (2) whatever is begotten must have a beginning of existence; (3) therefore, the Son had a beginning, which means he was not always. The conclusion is logically valid given his premises — but the second premise is wrong. *Begotten* in theology describes a **relationship**, not an origin. The eternal generation of the Son does not describe a moment when he came into being; it describes an eternal, timeless relation between Father and Son.

Arius spread his teaching through catchy songs — the ancient equivalent of going viral — and his theology spread rapidly throughout the Eastern church. Within a few decades, what had been declared a heresy was embraced by most of the empire, leaving Athanasius, bishop of Alexandria, essentially alone in defending the full deity of Christ. (*Athanasius contra mundum* — "Athanasius against the world.")

## The Council of Nicaea, 325 AD

Emperor Constantine, who had recently ended official persecution of Christianity and moved the imperial capital to Constantinople, called the Council of Nicaea in 325 to resolve the controversy. Approximately 318 bishops attended — most from the Greek-speaking East, with only seven from the West.

Many of the attending bishops still bore in their bodies the marks of the persecution that had ended just twenty years earlier — missing eyes, missing limbs, scars from beatings. This was not an abstract debate to them.

Three parties arrived at Nicaea:
- **The anti-Arian party**, led by Alexander of Alexandria and his young archdeacon Athanasius.
- **The pro-Arian party**, led by the influential Eusebius of Nicomedia, a friend of the emperor.
- **The middle party** — the largest group — led by Eusebius of Caesarea, who wanted everyone to get along. He proposed language saying the Son was *similar* (*homoios*) to the Father. Every Arian could sign that paper, because "similar" leaves room for something less than full equality.

The anti-Arian party objected. Hoseus of Cordoba, Constantine's own theological advisor, proposed a single letter change: not *homoios* (similar essence) but *homoousios* (same essence). That single letter — the Greek iota — made all the difference. An Arian could not sign a document saying the Son is of the same essence as the Father.

Words matter. Individual letters matter. The council approved *homoousios*, declared Arianism a heresy on June 19, 325, and exiled those who held it.

## The Settlement Did Not Last

Constantine's son became emperor and had been raised on Arian songs. He reversed his father's policy, recalled the Arians, and for the next sixty years Arianism was essentially the official religion of the empire. Athanasius was exiled five times for refusing to compromise.

Finally, Constantine's grandson Theodosius called another council in Constantinople in 381. The bishops reaffirmed the theology of Nicaea and expanded the creed to close any remaining loopholes. That is the text of the Nicene Creed we have today — a product of 381, not 325.

The conclusion of Arianism is logically the same conclusion reached by Jehovah's Witnesses today: one greater God and one lesser god called the Son. Arius thought he was protecting monotheism, but he ended up with multiple gods — the very thing he feared. Orthodoxy insists: there is one God, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, equal in glory, power, and substance, three distinct persons in one being.

## Why It Still Matters

You may have noticed the word "Catholic" in the Nicene Creed: "We believe in one holy Catholic and apostolic church." The small "c" in our translation signals that this means *universal* — the worldwide church of Jesus Christ that holds to the teaching of the apostles. The church did not begin at the Reformation. The Reformation was a recovery of apostolic doctrine that the Roman church had obscured. The visible church of every era is the same church — the body of Christ, continuous from Abraham through today.

The Nicene Creed will be studied in depth over the coming weeks.
